nodes.title: Prepare settle functions in outer scope
nodes.why: These references are filled once inside the Promise constructor, then exposed as the createDeferred() controls.
nodes.alternative: Do not expose settle functions at all.
nodes.tradeoff: External control is useful for deterministic async tests, but it can be risky in production flows.

nodes.title: Create the Promise once
nodes.why: The returned { promise, resolve, reject } object should manage one promise instance with one lifecycle.
nodes.alternative: Create a new Promise every time resolve/reject is called.
nodes.tradeoff: A single promise preserves native pending and first-settlement-wins behavior, but each deferred is one-shot.

nodes.title: Capture `resolve` from executor
nodes.why: This lets tests decide exactly when the promise should fulfill and allows native Promise adoption when resolving with another Promise.
nodes.tradeoff: More control for tests, but timing now depends on the caller invoking resolve.

nodes.title: Capture `reject` from executor
nodes.why: Tests can reject with an exact reason without waiting for real network or timers.
nodes.alternative: Throw errors in helper code instead of rejecting.
nodes.tradeoff: Explicit rejection is realistic for async APIs, but callers must handle rejections.

nodes.title: Return the deferred contract
nodes.why: This is the createDeferred() contract: return the promise with its resolve(value) and reject(error) controls.
nodes.alternative: Return an array tuple like [promise, resolve, reject].
nodes.tradeoff: Object return is self-describing and matches the challenge tests.
